title: Polar and quadratic magneto-optical Kerr effects in nonmagnetic/ferromagnet bilayers for spin-orbit torque measurements
authors: Yukihiro Marui, Masashi Kawaguchi, Kohji Nakamura, Masamitsu Hayashi
abstract: Recent studies have revealed that spin Hall magnetoresistance (SMR) contributes to both the anomalous and planar Hall resistances in nonmagnetic metal (NM)/ferromagnetic metal (FM) bilayers. This effect becomes pronounced when the NM layer exhibits a large spin Hall angle, as in W/CoFeB bilayers. In such systems, the ratio of planar to anomalous Hall resistances, normally small in single CoFeB layers, can approach unity. This unusually large ratio complicates the determination of spin-torque efficiency using harmonic Hall voltage measurements. To overcome this limitation, magneto-optical Kerr effect (MOKE) measurements have been proposed as an alternative approach. Here, we investigate the polar and quadratic MOKE components, which correspond to, respectively, the anomalous and planar Hall resistances in the low-frequency limit to clarify whether the MOKE measurements are suitable for characterizing the spin-torque efficiency. We find that the ratio of quadratic to polar MOKE signals in NM/FM bilayers is significantly smaller than the corresponding Hall resistance ratio, indicating that SMR contributes negligibly to the MOKE response in the visible range. Consequently, the spin-torque efficiency extracted from MOKE measurements agree well with those expected from the spin Hall angle of the NM layer. These results clarify the reason why MOKE measurements provide reliable determination of the spin-torque efficiency.
summary: This work compares two methods—Hall resistance measurements and MOKE spectroscopy—to measure spin-torque efficiency in magnetic bilayers. The authors demonstrate that the MOKE signal's quadratic component is largely immune to SMR effects, unlike the planar Hall resistance. This allows MOKE to provide a robust and reliable determination of fundamental spintronic parameters.
main_problem: The study aims to determine if Magneto-Optical Kerr Effect (MOKE) measurements provide a reliable alternative to electrical transport methods for characterizing spin-torque efficiency in nonmagnetic/ferromagnetic bilayers.
main_result: The ratio of quadratic to polar MOKE signals is found to be significantly smaller than the corresponding Hall resistance ratio, indicating that Spin Hall Magnetoresistance (SMR) contributes negligibly to the MOKE response. Consequently, MOKE measurements yield reliable estimates for spin-torque efficiency.
method: The authors compare electrical transport measurements (Anomalous/Planar Hall resistances, second harmonic voltage) with optical MOKE measurements (polar and quadratic components of Kerr effect).
model_or_system: The research focuses on Nonmagnetic metal (NM)/Ferromagnetic metal (FM) bilayers, such as W/CoFeB and Ta/CoFeB. The analysis involves modeling the magnetization response under external fields and applied currents.
key_observables: Planar and anomalous Hall resistances ($R_P, R_A$), polar ($\eta_P$) and quadratic ($\eta_Q$) MOKE components, and spin-torque efficiencies ($\xi_{DL}, \xi_{FL}$).
important_parameters: The ratio $r_\eta = \eta_Q/\eta_P$ is a key diagnostic; the failure of electrical methods occurs when the Hall resistance ratio approaches unity.
main_assumptions: Key assumptions include neglecting certain terms in the low-field approximation and assuming that MOKE measurements are insensitive to SMR effects, unlike planar Hall resistance.
figures_summary: Figures show field dependence of Hall resistance ($R_{yx}$) vs. angle ($\varphi_H$), and how both electrical (second harmonic voltage) and optical ($\eta_K$) signals vary with applied magnetic field amplitude ($H$).
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the problem—the complication caused by SMR in Hall measurements—and then proposing MOKE as a solution. It details the theoretical framework for both electrical and optical measurements, presents comparative results showing the discrepancy between methods when $r_H \approx 1$, and concludes that MOKE provides consistent results.
why_interesting: While not directly related to quantum computation, the rigorous comparison of multiple measurement techniques (electrical vs. optical) to isolate fundamental physical parameters like spin-torque efficiency is highly relevant in advanced condensed matter physics research concerning spintronics and magnetodynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic illustration of the experimental setup and definitions of the coordinate axis, the longitudinal Vxx and Hall Vyx voltages, and the real θK and imaginary ηK com- ponents of the MOKE signal. w and L are, respectively, the width and length of the current channel of the Hall bar. The polarization of the incident and reflected light are sketched.
Figure 2. Schematic drawing of the optical setup. BS: beam splitter, HWP: half-wave plate, QWP: quarter-wave plate, PBS: polarized beam splitter, BPD: balanced photodetector.

Figure 3. (a,c) The out of plane field Hz dependence of the Hall resistance Ryx. (b,d) Ryx plotted against the in-plane magnetic field angle φH. The field amplitude is 1.7 T. The solid lines show fit to the data with a sinusoidal function. (a,b) Ta/CoFeB, (c,d) W/CoFeB.

Figure 4. (a-d) The in-plane field Hx dependence of the Hall resistance Ryx (a,c) and Kerr ellipticity ηK (b,d). The solid lines represent fit to the data with a parabolic function. Ryx here is obtained using the AC electrical measurements, that is, Ryx = Rω. The error bars in (b,d) represent the standard deviation of the MOKE signal over multiple measurements. (a,b) Ta/CoFeB, (c,d) W/CoFeB.
Figure 5. sin(2φ) dependence of ˜rH (a,c) and ˜rη (b,d). (a,b) Ta/CoFeB, (c,d) W/CoFeB. The error bars in (b,d) represent errors of fitting Eq. (11) to the data.
